event: Portuguese navigators João de Santarém and Pedro Escobar reach the previously uninhabited island of São Tomé.
The Portuguese sight and name the island of Príncipe, completing the discovery of the archipelago.
King John II of Portugal grants the captaincy of São Tomé to João de Paiva, formally beginning attempts at settlement.
Álvaro de Caminha establishes the first permanent and successful settlement on São Tomé, partly populated by exiled convicts and Jewish children.
Príncipe is granted as a captaincy and begins to be settled, extending Portuguese control across the archipelago.
Sugar cane cultivation expands rapidly using enslaved African labor, making the islands a major early plantation economy.
São Tomé and Príncipe come under direct Portuguese Crown administration as a royal colony.
São Tomé becomes one of the world's leading sugar producers and a key entrepôt in the transatlantic slave trade.
The town of São Tomé is elevated to city status and a Catholic bishopric is established, making it a regional religious center.
Major slave revolts led by figures such as Amador disrupt the plantations, contributing to the decline of the sugar industry.
A large-scale slave uprising led by Amador devastates plantations, with rebels briefly controlling much of the island before being suppressed.
Dutch forces attack and briefly occupy São Tomé, reflecting the islands' vulnerability amid European rivalries.
The Dutch West India Company captures and occupies São Tomé, holding it until Portuguese forces retake it in 1648.
The colonial capital is moved from the city of São Tomé to Santo António on Príncipe due to instability and disease.
Santo António on Príncipe is confirmed as the administrative capital of the colony for much of the century.
Coffee is introduced to the islands, beginning to revive the plantation economy after the long decline of sugar.
Cacao (cocoa) is introduced to São Tomé, a crop that would transform the islands' economy in the following century.
The capital is moved back from Príncipe to the city of São Tomé, reflecting renewed economic focus on the main island.
Slavery is formally abolished in the Portuguese colony, though forced labor practices continue under new guises.
Full emancipation takes effect, but plantation owners replace enslaved people with contract (serviçais) workers from Angola and Mozambique.
São Tomé becomes the world's largest producer of cocoa, driving an export boom built on coerced contract labor.
British chocolate firms led by Cadbury boycott São Tomé cocoa over the use of forced labor amounting to slavery.
Portuguese authorities and planters massacre hundreds of Forros (Creole islanders) in the Batepá massacre, a defining trauma of colonial rule.
Nationalists found the Committee for the Liberation of São Tomé and Príncipe, the forerunner of the independence movement.
The independence movement is reorganized as the Movement for the Liberation of São Tomé and Príncipe (MLSTP), led by Manuel Pinto da Costa.
Portugal's Carnation Revolution overthrows the dictatorship, opening the way for decolonization of its African territories.
São Tomé and Príncipe gains independence on 12 July, with Manuel Pinto da Costa becoming the country's first president.
The newly independent state is admitted to the United Nations and the Organisation of African Unity.
Prime Minister Miguel Trovoada is arrested amid a power struggle, and the country consolidates as a one-party socialist state under the MLSTP.
A new constitution approved by referendum ends one-party rule and establishes multiparty democracy.
Miguel Trovoada wins the country's first free multiparty presidential election.
Príncipe is granted political and administrative autonomy as an autonomous region within the country.
A brief military coup briefly detains President Trovoada before being resolved without bloodshed through international mediation.
Businessman Fradique de Menezes is elected president, succeeding Miguel Trovoada.
Soldiers stage a short-lived coup while President de Menezes is abroad, but he is restored to power within days.
São Tomé and Príncipe and Nigeria establish a Joint Development Zone to explore promising offshore oil reserves in the Gulf of Guinea.
Former president Manuel Pinto da Costa returns to power, winning the presidency as an independent candidate.
Evaristo Carvalho is declared president after Pinto da Costa withdraws from a contested runoff election.
São Tomé and Príncipe cuts ties with Taiwan and re-establishes diplomatic relations with the People's Republic of China.
Carlos Vila Nova is elected president, continuing the country's tradition of peaceful democratic transitions.
An attempted coup at the army headquarters in São Tomé is repelled, with several detainees later reported killed in custody.
